content.problemStatement:
Chef has a safe with NNN dials. The iii-th dial initially shows the digit AiA_iAi​.

To unlock the safe, the digit shown on the iii-th dial should be BiB_iBi​.

In order to achieve this, Chef can choose a dial and perform one of the following two moves on it:


UP move: Increases the number on the chosen dial by 111. If it is 999 then it becomes 111.
DOWN move: Decreases the number on the chosen dial by 111. If it is 111 then it becomes 999.
In order to not seem too suspicious, Chef thinks he has to use **exactly** KKK moves to unlock the safe.

Is it possible for Chef to unlock the safe while performing exactly KKK moves in the process?

Note that Chef is allowed to reach the target digits even before KKK moves: however, even if he does, he must still continue making moves till he has made exactly KKK moves - it is not allowed to stop before that.

See the example cases below for more clarity.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the number of dials and the exact number of moves allowed.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​  — the initial digits on the dials.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​  — the target digits on the dials.




Output Format
For each test case, print the answer on a new line: `YES` if Chef can unlock the safe after using exactly KKK moves, and `NO` otherwise.


You may print each character of the answer in either uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.output:
No
Yes
No
Yes
No
Yes
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: All three dials are different, but Chef wants to unlock in two moves. This is not possible, since only one dial can be changed in one move - so after two moves at least one dial will be incorrect.
Test case 222: One sequence of K=7K = 7K=7 moves is as follows:

UP on the first dial, now the sequence is [2,2,1][2, 2, 1][2,2,1].
DOWN on the second dial, now the sequence is [2,1,1][2, 1, 1][2,1,1].
UP on the third dial two times, now the sequence is [2,1,3][2, 1, 3][2,1,3].
DOWN on the third dial three times, now the sequence is [2,1,9][2, 1, 9][2,1,9], and we've used 1+1+2+3=71+1+2+3 = 71+1+2+3=7 moves as needed.

Test case 333: We already have Ai=BiA_i = B_iAi​=Bi​ for every iii initially. However, we need to perform a move, which will break that equality.
Test case 444: We have Ai=BiA_i = B_iAi​=Bi​ for every iii initially, and need to make 999 moves. Simply perform an UP move on the first dial 999 times, and it will end up back where it started.
